content:
The expressions of fuel cost of two thermal generating units as a function of the respective power generation $${P_{G1}}$$ and $${P_{G2}}$$ are given as

$$\matrix{ {{F_1}({P_{G1}}) = 0.1aP_{G1}^2 + 40{P_{G1}} + 120\,Rs/hour} & {0\,MW \le {P_{G1}} \le 350\,MW} \cr {{F_2}({P_{G2}}) = 0.2P_{G2}^2 + 30{P_{G2}} + 100\,Rs/hour} & {0\,MW \le {P_{G2}} \le 300\,MW} \cr } $$

where $$a$$ is a constant. For a given value of $$a$$, optimal dispatch requires the total load of 290 MW to be shared as $${P_{G1}} = 175\,MW$$ and $${P_{G2}} = 115\,MW$$. With the load remaining unchanged, the value of $$a$$ is increased by 10% and optimal dispatch is carried out. The changes in $${P_{G1}}$$ and the total cost of generation, $$F( = {F_1} + {F_2})$$ in Rs/hour will be as follows

options.content: $${P_{G1}}$$ will decrease and $$F$$ will increase
Both $${P_{G1}}$$ and $$F$$ will increase
$${P_{G1}}$$ will increase and $$F$$ will decrease
Both $${P_{G1}}$$ and $$F$$ will decrease

explanation:
Given :

$$ \begin{aligned} & F_1\left(P_{g_1}\right)=0.1 a P_{G_1}^2+40 P_{G_1}+120 \mathrm{Rs} / \mathrm{hr} \\\\ & F_2\left(P_{g_2}\right)=0.2 P_{G_2}^2+30 P_{G_2}+100 \mathrm{Rs} / \mathrm{hr} \end{aligned} $$

Incremental cost, $I_{C_1}=\frac{d F_1}{d P_{G_1}}=0.2 a P_{G_1}+40$

$$ \begin{aligned} & I_{C_2}=\frac{d F_2}{d P_{G_2}}=0.4 P_{G_2}+30 \\\\ & 0.2 a \times 175+40=0.4 \times 115+30 \end{aligned} $$

$$ a=1.028 $$

Now, $P_1^{\prime}+P_2^{\prime}=175+115=290 \mathrm{~mW}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & a^{\prime}=1.1 \times 1.028=1.1308 \\\\ & F_1=0.1 \times 1.1308 P_1^{\prime 2}+40 P_1{ }^{\prime}+120 \\\\ & F_2=0.2 P_2^{\prime 2}+30 P_2+100 \\\\ & I_{C_1}=0.226 P_1{ }^{\prime}+40 \\\\ & I_{C_2}=0.4 P_2{ }^{\prime}+30 \end{aligned} $$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { For optimal load, } I_{C_1}=I_{C_2} \\\\ & 0.226 P_1^{\prime}+40=0.4 P_2^{\prime}+30 \\\\ & 0.226 P_1^{\prime}-0.4 P_2^{\prime}=-10 ......(i)\end{aligned}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & P_1^{\prime}+P_2^{\prime}=290 \\\\ & P_1^{\prime}=169 \mathrm{MW} \\\\ & P_2^{\prime}=121 \mathrm{MW} \end{aligned} $$

So, $P_1$ decreases

$F$ when $P_1=175 \mathrm{MW}$ and $P_2=115 \mathrm{MW}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & F_1=0.1 \times 1.028 \times 175^2+40 \times 175+120=10268.2 \mathrm{Rs} / \mathrm{hr} \\\\ & F_2=0.2 \times 115^2+30 \times 115+100=6195 \mathrm{Rs} / \mathrm{hr} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & F=F_1+F_2=16463 \mathrm{Rs} / \mathrm{hr} \\\\ & F_1^{\prime}=0.1 \times 1.1308 \times 169^2+40 \times 169+120=10109.6 \\\\ & F_2^{\prime}=0.2 \times 121^2+30 \times 121+100=6658.2 \\\\ & F^{\prime}=F_1^{\prime}+F_2^{\prime}=16767.8 \mathrm{Rs} / \mathrm{hr} \end{aligned} $$

So, $F$ increase

Hence, the correct option is (A).

The bus admittance ($$Y_{bus}$$) matrix of a 3-bus power system is given below.

$$\quad\quad$$$$\matrix{ 1 & \quad\quad\quad2\quad\quad & 3 \cr } $$

$$\matrix{ 1 \cr 2 \cr 3 \cr } \left[ {\matrix{ { - j15} & {j10} & {j5} \cr {j10} & { - j13.5} & {j4} \cr {j5} & {j4} & { - j8} \cr } } \right]$$

Considering that there is no shunt inductor connected to any of the buses, which of the following can NOT be true?

options.content: Line charging capacitor of finite value is present in all three lines
Line charging capacitor of finite value is present in line 2-3 only
Line charging capacitor of finite value is present in line 2-3 only and shunt capacitor of finite value is present in bus 1 only
Line charging capacitor of finite value is present in line 2-3 only and shunt capacitor of finite value is present in bus 3 only

The p.u. parameters for a $$500$$ MVA machine on its own base are:
Inertia, M = $$20$$ p.u.; reactance X = $$2$$ p.u. The p.u. values of inertia and reactance on $$100$$ MVA common base, respectively are

The three-bus power system shown in the figure has one alternator connected to bus 2 which supplies 200 MW and 40 MVAr power. Bus 3 is infinite bus having a voltage of magnitude $$|{V_3}| = 1.0$$ p.u. and angle of $$-15^\circ$$. A variable current source, $$|I|\angle \phi $$ is connected at bus 1 and controlled such that the magnitude of the bus 1 voltage is maintained at 1.05 p.u. and the phase angle of the source current, $$\phi = {\theta _1} \pm {\pi \over 2}$$, where $$\theta_1$$ is the phase angle of the bus 1 voltage. The three buses can be categorized for load flow analysis as

options.content:
$$Bus~1~~Slack~bus$$

$$Bus~2~~P-|V|~bus$$

$$Bus~3~~P-Q~bus$$

$$Bus~1~~P-|V|~bus$$

$$Bus~2~~P-|V|~bus$$

$$Bus~3~~Slack~bus$$

$$Bus~1~~P-Q~bus$$

$$Bus~2~~P-Q~bus$$

$$Bus~3~~Slack~bus$$

$$Bus~1~~P-|V|~bus$$

$$Bus~2~~P-Q~bus$$

$$Bus~3~~Slack~bus$$

explanation:
Bus 1 is classified as a PV bus because its voltage magnitude is maintained at 1.05 p.u. and the phase angle of the source current is adjusted to be at right angles to its voltage phase angle.

Bus 2 is classified as a PQ bus since it is an alternator connected to it, supplying 200 MW and 40 MVAr.

Bus 3 is considered a Slack bus because it is an infinite bus with a specified voltage magnitude of 1.0 p.u. and an angle of -15°.

Thus, the categorization of the buses for the load flow analysis is as follows:

  • Bus 1: PV bus

  • Bus 2: PQ bus

  • Bus 3: Slack bus

Therefore, the correct option is (D).
